inner_outer_monologue.description: Alternating between speaking the written text of a monologue and speaking aloud the actual inner life (feelings, thoughts, impulses) of each moment, then carrying that inner life into the next written line.
inner_outer_monologue.instrumental_use: Builds trust in the impulsive flow and frees the actor to express all inner realities without judgment.
inner_outer_monologue.craft_use: Integrates moment-to-moment inner truth with the written text, so the words become expressions of actual feelings rather than performed lines.
inner_outer_monologue.when_to_use: During preparation and rehearsal of any monologue. Excellent for actors who feel disconnected from their text.

sources.summary: Go back and forth between the written text and the inner life of the moment. Carry emotional life from inner monologue into the written piece.
Written words become expression of inner truth. Impulses expressed, even if not right for material, should be accepted — adjustments can be made later.

primal_moan.name: Primal Moan
primal_moan.description: Lying in the fetal position, the actor makes a deep moaning sound from the deepest possible place, holding it on a single breath and repeating it for about five minutes, allowing any emotional response — including heavy sobbing — to occur.
primal_moan.instrumental_use: Directly accesses the unconscious; produces emotional dimensions and colors no other exercise stimulates. Makes the actor infinitely more emotionally available afterward. Often produces ultimate-consciousness experiences.
primal_moan.craft_use: Used before rehearsal or performance to open the emotional instrument. Can be returned to if the actor 'dries up' during a monologue. Most powerful when followed by Primitive Abandonment.
primal_moan.when_to_use: As a preparation before work on material, especially for scenes requiring deep emotional life. Can be used whenever emotional access is blocked.

sources.summary: Lie in fetal position, make deep moaning sound from the deepest place, hold on single breath, repeat for five minutes. Allow heavy sobbing if it occurs.
Acting work immediately after the primal moan is full of ultimate-consciousness life — incredible things happen.
Afterward, actor is infinitely more available. If you dry up during a monologue, return to it.

primitive_abandonment.name: Primitive Abandonment
primitive_abandonment.description: A primal exercise done lying, sitting, or standing in which the actor makes wild, primitive sounds and movements, escalating them into an animalistic, abandoned frenzy. Three approaches: (1) sounds and rhythm escalating freely; (2) starting with primitive movement and adding rhythm; (3) adopting an animal posture and exaggerating it into wild animal behavior.
primitive_abandonment.instrumental_use: Hooks into unconscious impulses; stimulates unexplainable, exhilarating primal responses that make the actor wild, animal, and impulsive.
primitive_abandonment.craft_use: Best done immediately following Primal Moan as part of the Ultimate-Consciousness Trio. The primitive energy carries over into acting work.
primitive_abandonment.when_to_use: As preparation before deep emotional work on material. Works best following Primal Moan.

sources.summary: Done like conventional Abandonment but from a lying, sitting, or standing position. Stimulates unexplainable, exhilarating impulses.
Three approaches all lead to the same place: wild, animal, primitive, impulsive. Choose based on which works better for the individual actor.

switch_trick_fear_terror.name: Switch-Trick Exercise in Fear and Terror Areas
switch_trick_fear_terror.description: Rapid-fire suggestions of terrifying stimuli — things, people, places, and conditions from one's real fears and nightmares — with responses expressed aloud after each 'Switch!' command.
switch_trick_fear_terror.instrumental_use: Connects the actor to survival mechanisms, instincts, and insights from the unconscious that originate in deep fear. Stimulates raw, uncontrolled emotional responses. Direct link to nightmare/dream material = direct link to the unconscious.
switch_trick_fear_terror.craft_use: Used to access genuine terror, helplessness, and primal responses needed for extreme scenes. Part of the Ultimate-Consciousness Trio.
switch_trick_fear_terror.when_to_use: As part of the Ultimate-Consciousness Trio (after Primal Moan and Primitive Abandonment). Can also be done independently when terror is required.

sources.summary: Rapid suggestions of real fears — dark, knife, snake — expressed out loud. Works sitting or standing.
Connects to unconscious survival mechanisms. If using nightmare material, creates direct link to the unconscious.

ultimate_consciousness_trio.name: Ultimate-Consciousness Trio
ultimate_consciousness_trio.description: A three-part sequence of exercises done consecutively: Primal Moan → Primitive Abandonment → Switch-Trick in Fear Areas, each building on the momentum of the last without interruption.
ultimate_consciousness_trio.instrumental_use: The most powerful sequence for stimulating ultimate-consciousness experiences. The combination and sequence produce results beyond what any single exercise achieves alone.
ultimate_consciousness_trio.craft_use: Used before deep scene work when the actor needs maximum unconscious access and emotional availability.
ultimate_consciousness_trio.when_to_use: As an extended preparation (likely 20+ minutes) before demanding emotional scene work. Most effective when done in a private space without interruption.

sources.summary: Primal Moan → Primitive Abandonment → Switch-Trick, done consecutively. The cumulative momentum produces amazing things — thoughts and impulses from a very deep place.

animal_exploration.name: Animal Exploration
animal_exploration.description: Assuming an animal posture, moving like that animal, making animal sounds, and escalating the behavior into an abandonment-level frenzy. The goal is not accurate external embodiment of the animal but stimulation of primal, primitive impulses.
animal_exploration.instrumental_use: Opens the capillaries of the unconscious; stimulates primitive impulses buried inside. Produces wild, instinctive emotional life.
animal_exploration.craft_use: Done as part of Primitive Abandonment or as a standalone preparation. Can be followed by Personal Inventory to measure its impact on the unconscious.
animal_exploration.when_to_use: As part of the primal preparation sequence; when the actor needs to access primitive or instinctual emotional life for a role.

sources.summary: Adopt animal posture, move like the animal, exaggerate into wild, abandoned experience. Doesn't need accuracy — just primal stimulation.
Choose a specific animal or a conglomerate. Ease into it, then increase speed, size, sounds. Continue until genuinely animal-like and no longer thinking.
Animal explorations open the capillaries of the unconscious. Follow with Personal Inventory to measure the impact.

im_five_years_old.name: 'I'm Five Years Old and I...' Exercise
im_five_years_old.description: The actor states 'I'm five years old and I...' and then streams consciousness from that age, recalling memories of people, places, experiences, feelings. Then advances year by year or jumps to other key ages.
im_five_years_old.instrumental_use: Opens memory and frees forgotten emotions; unlocks unconscious storage of early life experiences. Makes the actor more vulnerable, affectable, and available. Also enriches dreams.
im_five_years_old.craft_use: Used as a craft tool for creating characters; also works as an instrumental exercise for emotional access and unconscious connection.
im_five_years_old.when_to_use: As a daily workout exercise; as a preparation before scene work. Best done in private with time to go deep.

sources.summary: Used for instrumental and craft reasons. Repeating it opens memory and frees forgotten emotions stored in the unconscious.
Example: 'I'm five years old and I live at 1347 Fillmore Street... I saw Frankenstein and can't sleep... I worry a lot...' Then moves to age six.
Practice repeatedly — establishes a connection with your past, creates comfort digging into past experiences, enriches dreams.

affective_memory_technique.name: Affective Memory
affective_memory_technique.description: Re-creating a past emotional experience through systematic sensory questioning — starting with the surrounding context hours before the emotional event, then sneaking up on the moment itself — so the original feelings recur organically.
affective_memory_technique.instrumental_use: Can stimulate ultimate-consciousness experiences when the memory is powerful enough. Opens the unconscious to supply inspiration beyond what was consciously chosen.
affective_memory_technique.craft_use: Choice approach #12 in Morris's system. Used to achieve specific emotional results required by a scene when no other approach reaches that emotional territory.
affective_memory_technique.when_to_use: For scenes requiring deep emotional life that the actor cannot access through other means. Particularly for extreme emotional states: grief, terror, profound loss.

sources.summary: Primary use: re-create past experiences to achieve emotional results needed for the scene. Reaches areas otherwise unavailable.
Ask all sensory questions in the here and now. Recreate everything surrounding the experience — place, time, people, objects — then sneak up on the moment.
Go back and forth between senses. The reality must be complete. The feelings recur from storage when the context is fully recreated.

personal_inventory_iii.name: Personal Inventory III
personal_inventory_iii.description: A deeper version of Personal Inventory in which the actor investigates the origins of feelings whose source is unclear — breaking down each sense individually to identify what is causing a particular emotional state.
personal_inventory_iii.instrumental_use: Creates greater self-awareness and sensitivity to unconscious influences already operating on the actor. Identifies triggers from the environment or from unconscious memory that may be creating unexplained emotional states.
personal_inventory_iii.craft_use: Helps the actor recognize when they are already being affected by unconscious stimuli that can be used or amplified for a scene.
personal_inventory_iii.when_to_use: When Personal Inventory reveals feelings whose origin is unknown. Break down each sense: smell, sight, sound, touch, taste.

sources.summary: If a feeling's origin is unknown, investigate through the senses — break down each sensory channel to find what's causing the unexplained emotional state.
Example: 'I feel anxious — what's causing it? I smell pine trees, wood smoldering... I then identify the music from a past testing situation triggering old anxiety.' Unconscious stimuli identified.

memory_lane_field_trips.name: Memory-Lane Field Trips and Explorations
memory_lane_field_trips.description: Visiting antique shops, thrift stores, swap meets, or other places filled with objects from the past — holding them, smelling them, listening to old sounds — allowing these objects to trigger unconscious memories and emotional responses.
memory_lane_field_trips.instrumental_use: Creates a liaison between conscious and unconscious through sensory contact with triggers from the past; makes the actor more connected to their history and inner emotional resources.
memory_lane_field_trips.craft_use: Provides a rich source of choices that can be used in future acting work — both specific choice material and general emotional availability.
memory_lane_field_trips.when_to_use: Whenever time permits — as a regular life practice for collecting acting resources and keeping the unconscious accessible.

sources.summary: Visit antique shops, thrift stores, swap meets — smell the odors, listen to old toys, feel objects. Allow them to affect you without analyzing.
Don't try to relate objects to specific incidents — just feel. The exploration creates a liaison between conscious and unconscious, and supplies future choices.

fragmented_images.name: Fragmented Images
fragmented_images.description: Rapidly switching between internally visualized images from the past — allowing each image to stimulate the next through association — creating an accelerating stream of memory and feeling.
fragmented_images.instrumental_use: Promotes a connection with the unconscious through visual association chains; stimulates memories and feelings not accessible through deliberate recall.
fragmented_images.craft_use: Can be done silently as a preparation or as a visualization exercise to access emotional material for a scene.
fragmented_images.when_to_use: As a preparation before scene work; as part of a daily workout routine.

sources.summary: Example: high school memories — 'Parking lot... blue Dodge... cafeteria... Parker pen... Spanish teacher...' — rapid-fire switch between images.
Done silently; images crystallize as you switch. Each image stimulates the next through association. Follow the path wherever it leads.

choice_hunt.name: Choice Hunt / Building a Repertoire of UC Choices
choice_hunt.description: A systematic practice of seeking, identifying, cataloguing, and testing choices that reliably access unconscious responses — building a personal repertoire for use in future acting work.
choice_hunt.instrumental_use: Conditions the unconscious to yield its resources more readily; ensures the actor has a library of proven UC choices available.
choice_hunt.craft_use: Provides specific choice material that can be matched to scene obligations requiring deep emotional life.
choice_hunt.when_to_use: Ongoing — as a daily practice of awareness. Keep a journal to record discoveries, underlining the strongest choices.

sources.summary: Identify and catalogue choices that stimulate UC responses. Know where to look — keep a journal of discoveries.
Collecting UC choices requires: awareness, consciousness of how things affect you, ability to identify a UC choice when encountered. Keep exploring fears — supply the stimulus sensorially.

being_workout_technique.name: Being Workout / Two-Person Being Workout
being_workout_technique.description: Two actors sit or stand face to face and communicate everything going on for them in the moment — acknowledging all feelings, obstacles, attractions, repulsions, and self-consciousness — until they reach a genuine being state together.
being_workout_technique.instrumental_use: Eliminates the obstacles that prevent genuine moment-to-moment relating. Frees both actors from the censoring of their authentic responses.
being_workout_technique.craft_use: Primary ensemble preparation tool. Can also be used as a standalone instrument-freeing exercise.
being_workout_technique.when_to_use: Before scene work as an ensemble preparation, or whenever both actors need to establish genuine contact before the material begins.

sources.summary: Both actors face each other and communicate what's happening in the moment — acknowledging obstacles aloud. Continue until genuinely comfortable.
Being Workout is listed as one of the core ensemble preparation exercises.

intimate_sharing.name: Intimate Sharing
intimate_sharing.description: Two actors share intimate, meaningful feelings about their real lives with each other — personal struggles, losses, fears, joys — deepening emotional exchange and creating genuine concern for each other.
intimate_sharing.instrumental_use: Produces genuine emotional depth and mutual concern; creates the foundation for moment-to-moment ensemble relating.
intimate_sharing.craft_use: Excellent preparation for scenes requiring deep personal emotional exchange. Creates real interdependence between actors.
intimate_sharing.when_to_use: As an ensemble preparation, especially when the scene requires a deep, trusting relationship between characters.

sources.summary: Actors share real intimate feelings about their lives. The deeper they get into personal experience, the fuller the results.
As concern for each other grows, involvement deepens. They respond more impulsively to each other — the foundation for ensemble.

imaginary_monologues.name: Imaginary Monologues
imaginary_monologues.description: One actor speaks aloud to an imagined person from their life (while the other actor listens and responds authentically) — the content is real and deeply personal, the addressee is imaginary.
imaginary_monologues.instrumental_use: Creates genuine emotional aliveness through the imagined conversation; the listening actor is affected by the reality of the speaker's emotion.
imaginary_monologues.craft_use: Also used as a choice approach — the actor speaks to a person from their life while saying the character's lines in a scene.
imaginary_monologues.when_to_use: As an ensemble preparation exercise; as a choice approach during scene work.

sources.summary: Each actor in turn does an imaginary monologue while the other listens and responds. Content must be emotionally important to the speaker.
Purpose: involve actors with each other on a meaningful level, promote impulsive responsiveness. The more involved, the more at stake, the better.

imaginary_dialogues.name: Imaginary Dialogues
imaginary_dialogues.description: Both actors simultaneously talk to their respective imaginary choices, responding to each other as if the other actor were or were related to their imagined person.
imaginary_dialogues.instrumental_use: Creates simultaneous dual involvement — the actor honors their own imaginary reality while remaining responsive to the other actor's energy. More complex than Imaginary Monologues.
imaginary_dialogues.craft_use: Excellent preparation for scenes with conflicting agendas or opposing emotional needs.
imaginary_dialogues.when_to_use: As an ensemble preparation; the imaginary choices should have some parallel to the scene's emotional demands.

sources.summary: Both actors simultaneously talk to imaginary choices. More complex — operating at possible cross purposes. Choices must not be diametrically opposed.
Actors can include each other's reality into their own to mend discrepancies. Choices should parallel scene demands.

you_make_me_feel.name: 'You Make Me Feel...' Exercise
you_make_me_feel.description: Actors take turns completing the phrase 'You make me feel...' and then responding to the response with 'That makes me feel...' or 'Now I feel...' — an honest, structured chain of moment-to-moment emotional reactions.
you_make_me_feel.instrumental_use: Piques genuine responses from reality; promotes an ongoing relating relationship of affecting and responding. Creates ensemble dependency.
you_make_me_feel.craft_use: Established ensemble can carry silently into scene work. After getting into choices, the exercise is continued silently under the lines, bridging preparation into the scene.
you_make_me_feel.when_to_use: As an ensemble preparation exercise before scene work; after instrumental preparations are complete.

sources.summary: Both actors face each other. Complete 'You make me feel...' and respond to the response. Creates a chain of authentic reactions.
After actors are well into it, drop the preface and just respond. Continue silently under lines to bridge into the scene.

looking_for_never_noticed.name: Looking for Things About the Other Person You Have Never Noticed Before
looking_for_never_noticed.description: Silently examining the other actor in detail, searching for features, qualities, and characteristics never previously noticed — color of eyes, shape of features, mannerisms, way of breathing.
looking_for_never_noticed.instrumental_use: Creates selfless interest in and genuine curiosity about the other person; breaks self-consciousness by focusing attention outward.
looking_for_never_noticed.craft_use: Beginning of the Ensemble Workout — adds sounds, then gibberish, then words as the exercise progresses.
looking_for_never_noticed.when_to_use: As the opening of an ensemble preparation, particularly when self-consciousness is a major obstacle.

sources.summary: Both actors remain silent and look for things they've never noticed — eyes, shape, mannerisms. Then add sounds, then gibberish, then words.
Each part 'piggybacks' the last — building depth and dependence. By the time words are used, ensemble should be established.

ensemble_workout.name: Ensemble Workout
ensemble_workout.description: A progressive ensemble exercise that begins with silent observation (Looking for Things Never Noticed), then adds sounds, then gibberish, then words — each level building on the last.
ensemble_workout.instrumental_use: Systematically builds from the simplest form of engagement to full verbal communication, ensuring each layer is grounded in genuine contact before the next is added.
ensemble_workout.craft_use: At the word stage, actors use the same content being expressed through their choices, creating a bridge into the material.
ensemble_workout.when_to_use: As a core ensemble preparation exercise; especially useful when actors have difficulty making the transition from exercise to scene.

sources.summary: Starts silently — looking for never-noticed things — then adds sounds, gibberish, words. Each part piggybacks the last.
By words stage, actors should have established the beginnings of ensemble. Each actor uses their choice material in the final stage.

working_for_choice_promoting_ensemble.name: Working for a Choice While Promoting Ensemble
working_for_choice_promoting_ensemble.description: After establishing ensemble through preparation exercises, the actor moves into their choice approach while simultaneously maintaining the ensemble relationship — going back and forth between the choice approach and the real, live actor in front of them.
working_for_choice_promoting_ensemble.instrumental_use: The ensemble relationship energizes the choice work; the choice work enriches the ensemble.
working_for_choice_promoting_ensemble.craft_use: The synthesis of process and ensemble — this is how the two become one in actual scene work. The actor jumps back and forth between evocative words, imaginary monologue, and the real actor.
working_for_choice_promoting_ensemble.when_to_use: After completing ensemble preparations and before or during scene performance.

sources.summary: Once ensemble basis is established, actors move to material carrying the ensemble relationship. Extremely important not to let it drop when moving to material.
Actor jumps between evocative words (choice approach) and responding to the real actress — intermingling them so the two people become one.

obligation_choice_chart_technique.name: Building the Obligation-Choice-and-Choice-Approach Chart
obligation_choice_chart_technique.description: Systematically identifying each obligation of a piece of material and mapping it to (1) a specific choice the actor will work for, and (2) the choice approach to be used to create that choice — creating a visual blueprint of the entire craft process.
obligation_choice_chart_technique.craft_use: Provides clarity about what must be fulfilled in each scene and a structured plan for how to do it. Evolves through the rehearsal process as choices are tested, verified, or replaced.
obligation_choice_chart_technique.when_to_use: From the beginning of any rehearsal process. Updated throughout.

sources.summary: The chart maps obligations to choices and approaches, creating a visual blueprint of the craft process.
Mark verified choices with an underline; cross out discarded ones (but don't obliterate them). Write alternates immediately below discards.

character_background_journal_technique.name: Creating a Character Background Journal
character_background_journal_technique.description: Writing a detailed first-person journal as the character — mixing real autobiographical material with invented parallel circumstances — to build a full interior prior life that can be drawn on throughout the rehearsal process.
character_background_journal_technique.instrumental_use: Opens unconscious memories and emotions tied to parallel experiences, enriching the actor's instrument.
character_background_journal_technique.craft_use: Creates the foundation for all character choices; makes character behavior feel grounded in a real history. Essential for characters whose inner life drives the play.
character_background_journal_technique.when_to_use: Early in the rehearsal process — before beginning specific choice work. Returns to it in each rehearsal.

sources.summary: Mix real background with imaginary parallel circumstances. Like Believability within a written scenario.
Must be used in every rehearsal — not just written once.

sub_personalities_facilitation.name: Sub-Personality Facilitation
sub_personalities_facilitation.description: Identifying the sub-personality that controls or most influences the character, then using journal conversation, specific choices, or facilitation exercises to elicit and elevate that sub-personality.
sub_personalities_facilitation.instrumental_use: Connects the actor to deep psychological energies within themselves, enriching availability.
sub_personalities_facilitation.craft_use: One of the most dimensional approaches to characterization. When the right sub-personality is found, it can fulfill multiple character element obligations simultaneously.
sub_personalities_facilitation.when_to_use: When a character is strongly controlled by a recognizable psychological energy. Identify the sub-personality from the text, then use journal work or specific choices to access it.

sources.summary: Get familiar with your sub-personalities — they form a repertoire for characterization. Identify which one controls the character.
To elicit a specific sub-personality: have a journal conversation with it, or work for choices that appeal to and elicit that particular energy.

journal_log_technique.name: Journal Log
journal_log_technique.description: Keeping a detailed per-rehearsal record of choices explored, approaches tried, results observed, discoveries made, and failures encountered — including specific notes on what to address in the next rehearsal.
journal_log_technique.instrumental_use: Develops the actor's metacognitive awareness of their own process.
journal_log_technique.craft_use: Provides the blueprint for each subsequent rehearsal; prevents repetition of failed approaches; tracks the evolution of choices.
journal_log_technique.when_to_use: After every rehearsal. Updated and referred to at the start of the next rehearsal.

sources.summary: Journal Log records what was explored, what was tried, what worked and didn't, what the next rehearsal should address.
Describing failures is as important as successes. We learn from failing and discover where choices lead.

sharing_monologue_approach.name: Sharing Monologue (Choice Approach #23)
sharing_monologue_approach.description: Speaking aloud or silently about real experiences from one's own life, selectively emphasizing elements that parallel the emotional obligation of the scene, to create or maintain the desired emotional state.
sharing_monologue_approach.instrumental_use: The emotional sharing itself can free the instrument from obstacles by expressing real feelings.
sharing_monologue_approach.craft_use: A flexible approach usable both as a preparation before a scene and as an ongoing internal approach during the scene. Works with a wide range of emotional obligations.
sharing_monologue_approach.when_to_use: Before scenes requiring a sustained emotional state; particularly effective for depression, joy, nostalgia, grief, or any state that can be stimulated by recalling parallel real experiences.

sources.summary: Emerged from what actors were already doing naturally. Formal recognition as choice approach #23.
Selectively emphasize the depressing elements in your life to promote depression. Can be done aloud or silently.

selective_enhancement_approach.name: Selective Enhancement (Choice Approach #24)
selective_enhancement_approach.description: Taking a real element in the scene — the quality of the other actor, a sensory element, an object — and sensorially amplifying it beyond its actual properties through sensory questions and responses.
selective_enhancement_approach.instrumental_use: The process of sensory augmentation deepens the actor's involvement with and affection by the scene's realities.
selective_enhancement_approach.craft_use: Can be done in combination with inner monologue, Believability, Endowments, or other choice approaches. Adds depth and dimensionality to what the other actor actually offers.
selective_enhancement_approach.when_to_use: When the real elements in the scene are good but insufficient to create the full desired emotional response; when the actor needs to amplify existing stimuli.

sources.summary: Discovered after actor mistakenly called Selective Emphasis 'enhancement.' The distinction: emphasis highlights what is there; enhancement makes it bigger.
Example in Believability: both actors use Selective Enhancement to respond to real things the other does — amplifying them to create heightened romantic feelings.

evocative_images_approach.name: Evocative Images (Choice Approach #25)
evocative_images_approach.description: Silently and rapidly flashing successive visual images from one's personal past — without speaking — allowing each image to stimulate feelings that carry into the scene work.
evocative_images_approach.instrumental_use: The silent image work creates an internal emotional life that feeds outward expression without conceptual interference.
evocative_images_approach.craft_use: Used during scene work, interspersed with inner monologue and the lines of the play. Particularly effective for creating emotional subtext.
evocative_images_approach.when_to_use: During rehearsal and performance when a rich emotional subtext is needed; can be used alongside other choice approaches.

sources.summary: Silent successive images — the actor sees them internally, says nothing. Like Evocative Words but using images.
Example: flashing images of frustrated interactions with his wife while using Endowments and delivering lines — creating a layered emotional reality.

finding_character_clues.name: Finding the Clues to the Character
finding_character_clues.description: Systematically mining the text of the play from all four sources — author's stage directions/descriptions, other characters' references, the character's own dialogue/monologues, and the character's actions and interactions — to build a complete picture of the character.
finding_character_clues.instrumental_use: The research process builds the actor's genuine interest in and connection to the character.
finding_character_clues.craft_use: Provides the raw material for the Obligation-Choice-and-Choice-Approach Chart's character section. Identifies what must be fulfilled and which elements are most impelling.
finding_character_clues.when_to_use: At the beginning of the rehearsal process, before making any choices. Returns to the text repeatedly throughout rehearsal.

sources.summary: Author's words provide physical and psychological descriptions of characters — Williams on Blanche, Miller on Willy and Linda.
Other characters' dialogue reveals how the character appears to others and how they've changed — Biff and Happy discussing each other.
Actions and interactions reveal inner impulses — what a character does often contradicts what they say (Iago). Also physical habits (Queeg's ball bearings).

film_home_preparation.name: Film Home Preparation
film_home_preparation.description: The systematic preparation the actor does at home before arriving on a film set — identifying all obligations, beginning to create the place sensorially, working on character elements, exploring choice approaches — so that on-set work can begin efficiently.
film_home_preparation.instrumental_use: Conditions the emotional and sensory choices before the pressure of the set.
film_home_preparation.craft_use: Creates the foundation so that on-set rehearsal time can be used for refinement rather than discovery. Essential given that film rarely has extended rehearsal periods.
film_home_preparation.when_to_use: During the days/weeks before shooting begins; also each night before the next shooting day.

sources.summary: Work at home to create the place sensorially before being on the set. Do most of the preparatory work at home so that on-set work is more efficient.
Look for choice areas that are multidimensional — that connect to the unconscious and will reach into it with some regularity.

on_set_preparation.name: On-Set Preparation
on_set_preparation.description: The techniques an actor uses immediately before shooting a scene — creating the place sensorially, getting the other actor to prepare, doing whatever preparation is possible within the technical constraints of the set.
on_set_preparation.instrumental_use: Must happen quickly under time pressure. Needs to be efficient — know the choice and approach well enough to access it in the available time.
on_set_preparation.craft_use: The place can be created through sense memory in the actual set environment. Selectively emphasize elements of the real location that support the scene.
on_set_preparation.when_to_use: Immediately before shooting; also between takes to maintain or adjust the emotional state.

sources.summary: On the set: create the time and place sensorially in the actual location. Know the camera's frame lines and use them.
Getting the other actor to prepare with you is itself an art — feel it out, see if they are process-oriented.

ten_rehearsal_process.name: Ten-Rehearsal Building Process
ten_rehearsal_process.description: A structured sequence of ten rehearsals that progressively builds the actor's realities from initial exploration to performance-ready state — each rehearsal adding new layers of choice work.
ten_rehearsal_process.instrumental_use: Each rehearsal begins with instrumental preparation (relaxation-cluster, personal inventory, being/ensemble work) to free the instrument before craft work begins.
ten_rehearsal_process.craft_use: Each rehearsal focuses on different obligations and choice combinations, tested and recorded in journals and charts. Later rehearsals consolidate and distill into a reliable, economical choice structure.
ten_rehearsal_process.when_to_use: For any two-person scene preparation in a workshop or class setting. Adapts to any material.

sources.summary: Ten sample rehearsals provide a blueprint for moving from first reading to performance readiness.
After ten rehearsals, work becomes simple — a single question replaces ten. Actors are ready to present the scene.
These ten rehearsals form a blueprint to follow. Alternatives are infinite — choices, approaches, and obligations vary with material.

vesuvius.name: Vesuvius
vesuvius.description: An exercise in which the actor vents all frustrations, feelings, and blocked impulses — expressing them with full intensity, possibly loudly and physically — to clear the blocks and return to a free state.
vesuvius.instrumental_use: Releases built-up emotional congestion; clears the instrument of frustration, anger, or any accumulated feeling that is blocking genuine availability.
vesuvius.craft_use: Used when the actor feels blocked in rehearsal — not to perform emotions but to actually release them so the instrument becomes available again.
vesuvius.when_to_use: When blocked in rehearsal; when tension or frustration is building and interfering with the work.

sources.summary: Actor does a 'Vesuvius,' expressing all frustrations when blocked in rehearsal. Used specifically to clear the instrument and restore access.

rehearsal_preparation_structure.name: Standard Rehearsal Preparation Structure
rehearsal_preparation_structure.description: A consistent sequential structure for beginning every rehearsal: (1) Relaxation-cluster; (2) Personal Inventory (aloud); (3) Share homework and discoveries; (4) Work on the next obligation; (5) Begin scene work from established preparation; (6) Record results in journal and charts.
rehearsal_preparation_structure.instrumental_use: The opening instrumental preparations ensure the actor arrives at the craft work in a free, available state rather than blocked by daily tensions.
rehearsal_preparation_structure.craft_use: The homework assignments connect one rehearsal to the next. The journal and chart recording provides the continuity blueprint.
rehearsal_preparation_structure.when_to_use: Every rehearsal, consistently. The ritual itself conditions the instrument to enter the creative state.

sources.summary: Rehearsal structure seen across multiple rehearsals: relaxation-cluster, personal inventory, share homework, create place, begin scene work.
Later rehearsals add: evocative words preparation, imaginary monologues, ensemble preparation, going into scene, recording results.

i_want_i_need.name: 'I Want, I Need' Preparation
i_want_i_need.description: An exercise in which the actor identifies and expresses aloud — in relation to the choice — everything they want and need from that person or situation, building emotional urgency and desire.
i_want_i_need.instrumental_use: Connects the actor to genuine desire and need rather than intellectual understanding, opening the emotional instrument.
i_want_i_need.craft_use: Creates a foundation of want and need in the character's relationship that drives the scene's action organically.
i_want_i_need.when_to_use: In rehearsal as a preparation when the character's desire for something is a central obligation.

sources.summary: Actress does 'I Want, I Need' preparation for the character obligation in rehearsal.
Used in rehearsal 10 as part of the preparation sequence.

accuse_and_indict.name: Accuse and Indict
accuse_and_indict.description: An exercise in which the actor expresses all grievances, accusations, and indictments to the choice (a real or imaginary person) — building emotional life through the expression of anger, hurt, and resentment.
accuse_and_indict.instrumental_use: Releases suppressed anger and hurt; creates genuine emotional energy from resentment and indignation.
accuse_and_indict.craft_use: Particularly useful for scenes of conflict, confrontation, or scenes where a character carries significant grievances against another.
accuse_and_indict.when_to_use: When an actor needs to access anger, outrage, or accumulated resentment for a scene. Often used in conjunction with Imaginary Monologues.

sources.summary: Actress uses 'Accuse-and-Indict exercise' to her boyfriend as part of rehearsal preparation for a conflict scene.

parallel_improvisation.name: Parallel Improvisation
parallel_improvisation.description: An improvisation in which the actors play out a scenario from their own real relationship history that parallels the situation in the play — using their own words and feelings while staying in the emotional territory of the material.
parallel_improvisation.instrumental_use: Creates genuine relational history and emotional resonance between the actors that can carry into the scene.
parallel_improvisation.craft_use: Builds the relationship foundation that gives the scene's written dialogue genuine weight. Especially useful for scenes about the history between characters.
parallel_improvisation.when_to_use: In middle rehearsals, after basic relationship work has been established. Particularly valuable for scenes about long-term relationships or specific past events.

sources.summary: Actors talk about things dating back to their first meeting — mutual friends, restaurants, early sexual feelings, first argument — as a parallel to what the characters might have shared.
Specific parallel improvisation: explore the beginning of the relationship, emphasizing the love and attraction, then subtly introduce the conflicts.

dream_suggestion_before_sleep.name: Encouraging Dreams About the Role
dream_suggestion_before_sleep.description: Deliberately reviewing the scene, choices, and character before sleep to encourage dreams about the work — and then paying attention to any suggestions that arrive from the sleep-wake state.
dream_suggestion_before_sleep.instrumental_use: Uses the unconscious processing of sleep to deepen and evolve the actor's creative work.
dream_suggestion_before_sleep.craft_use: Dreams about the work often contain insights, unexpected choices, or emotional revelations that enrich subsequent rehearsals.
dream_suggestion_before_sleep.when_to_use: Each night during a rehearsal period — particularly for complex or challenging material.

sources.summary: Go over the scene every night before bedtime using your choices. Encourage yourself to dream about the work, the play, and the specific choices.
Become aware of suggestions from your dreams or in the sleep-wake state.

cultivating_magical_child.name: Cultivating the Magical Child
cultivating_magical_child.description: Actively seeking and creating situations where the playful, imaginative, childlike inner self is expressed freely — through play, clowning, imagination exercises, and irreverent engagement with life.
cultivating_magical_child.instrumental_use: Reconnects the actor to their primary source of creative magic — the part that believes and plays without inhibition.
cultivating_magical_child.craft_use: The magical child is the foundation of all acting magic. Without it, no technique can produce inspired work.
cultivating_magical_child.when_to_use: As an ongoing life practice; actively seek moments of genuine play and imagination. Also through the fantasies and story-making exercises.

sources.summary: The magical child is always somewhere inside. How well you treat it determines how often it comes out. It needs to be actively cultivated.

process_trust.name: Trusting the Process
process_trust.description: Maintaining faith in the systematic craft process even when results are not immediately apparent — knowing that repetition, conditioning, and daily work build the pathways to inspired, organic acting.
process_trust.instrumental_use: Reduces the anxiety about 'doing it right' which is itself an obstacle to genuine being.
process_trust.craft_use: Allows the actor to explore and fail without panic — essential for the creative discovery that only comes from genuine risk-taking.
process_trust.when_to_use: Whenever the actor feels frustrated or blocked; whenever results seem elusive.

sources.summary: Acting is natural — once the instrument is free, it can act. We must open the doors. A dependable process enables magic consistently rather than by accident.
In art there are no guarantees. Artists do not sit down in front of a canvas and say 'Today this is going to be a masterpiece!' You explore. You discover what evolves from the choices.

curiosity_expansion_exercise.name: Curiosity Expansion Exercise
curiosity_expansion_exercise.goal: Permanently expand curiosity by training the habit of asking questions about everything in the environment.
curiosity_expansion_exercise.how_to: While waiting anywhere (restaurant, transit, office), glance around and ask questions about every object and person you see. Pursue things that hold no initial interest. Express curiosity about things arbitrarily. Repeat until it becomes a habit.
curiosity_expansion_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors; particularly those who feel disengaged or uninterested in the world.
curiosity_expansion_exercise.why_it_works: Curiosity atrophies from lack of use; deliberately exercising it re-awakens the faculty. Once habitual, it permanently expands consciousness.
curiosity_expansion_exercise.additional_info: Works best done everywhere, all the time. Even uninteresting objects become fascinating with sustained attention.

sources.summary: Ask questions about people, objects, and phenomena around you wherever you are. Arbitrarily get involved even with uninteresting things.
Riding in a car offers rich sensory field — practice curiosity there. Once habitual, consciousness permanently expands.

personal_point_of_view_exercise.name: Personal-Point-of-View Exercise
personal_point_of_view_exercise.goal: Develop sensitivity by putting the actor in contact with genuine feelings about objects in their environment and training them to express those feelings aloud.
personal_point_of_view_exercise.how_to: Sit in a room (or any environment). Look at each object, sound, or person and ask: 'What is my point of view about this? How do I feel about it?' Then express the feeling fully and audibly. Cover a wide range of objects. Continue for an extended time.
personal_point_of_view_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors, especially those who are emotionally blocked or who have difficulty connecting to moment-to-moment reality.
personal_point_of_view_exercise.why_it_works: By articulating honest reactions aloud, the actor strengthens the pathway between perception and emotional expression. Daily practice makes the process automatic.
personal_point_of_view_exercise.additional_info: Morris gives the example: responding to a phone (ambivalence), plants (love, attachment), neighbor's house (anger). The key is genuine, unexpurgated response.

sources.summary: Ask how you feel about each object and express it fully and audibly. Works best done daily.
Sample exercise includes phone, plants, neighbor house — honest emotional responses expressed aloud.

deductive_observation_exercise.name: Deductive Observation Exercise
deductive_observation_exercise.goal: Develop perception by training the actor to observe people and reason from observable evidence to deeper understanding.
deductive_observation_exercise.how_to: Go to a public place with many people. Choose individuals and start with simple observations ('briefcase = business'). Add deductions: age of suit, quality of food, posture, eye movements. Build increasingly complex hypotheses. Ask 'why' at each step.
deductive_observation_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors; especially useful for those who play characters they struggle to understand intuitively.
deductive_observation_exercise.why_it_works: Forces engagement with others and develops the perceptive muscle. Grows in sophistication with practice.
deductive_observation_exercise.additional_info: Can be done in restaurants, parks, airports, shopping centers. The goal is ever-increasing depth of observation and deduction.

sources.summary: Start with obvious observations, then add deductive layers. Works in any public place with variety of people.

dream_awareness_practice.name: Dream Awareness Practice
dream_awareness_practice.goal: Condition the actor to become increasingly aware of, remember, and record their dreams as a pathway to the unconscious.
dream_awareness_practice.how_to: Before sleep: decide firmly that you will be aware of dreaming. On waking from a dream: immediately record everything in a notebook or tape recorder — work fast before the dream dissolves. In the morning: replay the recording and process the content. Condition this as a daily practice.
dream_awareness_practice.who_should_do_it: All actors seeking to develop their unconscious connection. Most valuable for actors who feel their work lacks depth or dimensionality.
dream_awareness_practice.why_it_works: Dreams are the full expression of the unconscious. Engaging with them conditions the conscious-unconscious bridge — making unconscious material more accessible in performance.
dream_awareness_practice.additional_info: A tape recorder by the bed is very useful. The habit grows — over time, more complete dreams are remembered. The goal is not interpretation but emotional contact with the material.

sources.summary: Decide to be aware of dreaming. Record on waking using notebook or recorder.
Work fast — dreams dissolve rapidly. Condition this practice over time to retain more and more.

sense_memory_dream_reentry.name: Sense Memory Dream Re-Entry
sense_memory_dream_reentry.goal: Re-enter a recorded dream through sensory exploration to access the emotional life it contains and make it available for use in acting.
sense_memory_dream_reentry.how_to: With eyes closed (lying or sitting), start with the place of the dream. Ask sensory questions: 'What does this place look like? What color are the walls? What do I hear? Where are the sounds coming from?' Respond with the appropriate sense. Accept the dream's distorted logic. Proceed to people, events, and emotions in the dream.
sense_memory_dream_reentry.who_should_do_it: Any actor who has recorded a dream and wants to mine its emotional content.
sense_memory_dream_reentry.why_it_works: Sensory re-creation rebuilds the neural pathways of the dream experience, allowing emotions to resurface. The unusual logic of dreams accesses material unavailable to waking-state sense memory.
sense_memory_dream_reentry.additional_info: Accept distortions and illogical shifts — don't impose waking-state logic on the dream. This is what makes the experience uniquely rich.

sources.summary: Start with the place. Ask sensory questions in each modality. Accept the distortions and altered logic of the dream.

personal_inventory_exercise.name: Personal Inventory Exercise
personal_inventory_exercise.goal: Discover the actor's actual emotional and physical state in the moment before any acting work begins.
personal_inventory_exercise.how_to: Repeatedly ask 'How do I feel?' aloud or semi-audibly. Respond honestly in the moment after each question. Don't seek a particular answer — accept whatever is true. Continue until a clear picture of your current state emerges. Investigate causes of any feelings whose origin is unclear.
personal_inventory_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors; used as the starting point of any preparation. Essential if the actor feels disconnected, tense, or unsure of their emotional state.
personal_inventory_exercise.why_it_works: Most actors approach material without knowing where they actually are. Personal Inventory provides an honest snapshot that guides subsequent preparation choices.
personal_inventory_exercise.additional_info: Part of the six-step ensemble preparation sequence. Can be done simultaneously by both actors before a scene.

sources.summary: Sample: 'How do I feel?... I feel rested... anxious... irritated... out of sorts. What's causing that?' Investigate the origin of each feeling.
Step 1 in ensemble preparation — both actors do it simultaneously to find where they are.

primal_moan_exercise.name: Primal Moan Exercise
primal_moan_exercise.goal: Access the deepest emotional resources and establish a connection with unconscious impulses through primal sound-making.
primal_moan_exercise.how_to: Lie down on your side in fetal position — knees drawn up, hands between thighs. Begin making the deepest possible moaning sound from the most pained place inside. Hold each sound on a single breath. Repeat continuously. Allow any emotional response, including heavy sobbing. Continue for approximately five minutes, though shorter sessions are acceptable.
primal_moan_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors seeking to access deeper emotional life; actors who feel emotionally blocked or unavailable before deep scene work.
primal_moan_exercise.why_it_works: The fetal position and deep sound-making bypass intellectual control and activate primal emotional resources from the unconscious. The depth and color of emotion produced is unique — it seems to come from a different place entirely.
primal_moan_exercise.additional_info: The more affected the actor is, the more successful the exercise. If you 'dry up' during subsequent scene work, return to the exercise. Most powerful when followed immediately by Primitive Abandonment.

sources.summary: Fetal position. Deep moaning sound. Hold on single breath. Repeat for 5 minutes. Allow sobbing.

primitive_abandonment_exercise.name: Primitive Abandonment Exercise
primitive_abandonment_exercise.goal: Stimulate primitive, animal-level impulses and access unconscious energies through physical and vocal abandonment.
primitive_abandonment_exercise.how_to: Can be done lying, sitting, or standing. Choose one of three approaches: (1) Make wild, primitive sounds and let them evolve into rhythm and movement. (2) Start with primitive physical movement and add rhythm, escalating. (3) Adopt an animal posture and exaggerate it into wild animal behavior, adding sounds. In all cases, escalate to an abandoned frenzy.
primitive_abandonment_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors, especially before work requiring primal emotional energy. Most effective following Primal Moan.
primitive_abandonment_exercise.why_it_works: Bypasses intellectual control through physical and vocal primitivism. Stimulates unconscious energies and emotional states inaccessible through cognitive approaches.
primitive_abandonment_exercise.additional_info: Three approaches available — experiment to find which works best for you. Can be followed by Switch-Trick in Fear Areas as part of the Ultimate-Consciousness Trio.

sources.summary: Done like conventional Abandonment but from a variety of positions. Works individually or immediately after Primal Moan.
All three approaches lead to the same place: wild, animal, primitive, impulsive.

affective_memory_exercise.name: Affective Memory Exercise
affective_memory_exercise.goal: Re-create a past emotional experience through sensory means so that its original feelings recur organically and become available for use in acting.
affective_memory_exercise.how_to: Choose a powerful past experience (graduation, funeral, first love, a loss, a triumph, a trauma). Lie down with eyes closed. Start hours BEFORE the emotional event itself — recreate the surrounding context. Ask sensory questions in the here and now: 'What does this place look like? What color are the walls? What do I smell? What do I hear?' Respond with the actual sense — see, hear, feel, smell. Sneak up on the emotional moment through layers of sensory context.
affective_memory_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who need to access specific emotional states that other approaches cannot reach. Advanced actors comfortable with deep personal work.
affective_memory_exercise.why_it_works: By recreating the sensory context that surrounded an emotional experience, the actor rebuilds the neural pathways associated with that moment. The original emotional responses emerge organically when the context is sufficiently vivid.
affective_memory_exercise.additional_info: Do NOT approach the emotional moment directly — always approach through the surrounding context. Use as many sensory questions as needed for each element. Excellent choices include: funerals, first loves, meaningful losses, dramatic confrontations, peak experiences.

sources.summary: Re-create everything surrounding the experience. Ask sensory questions in the here and now. Sneak up on the moment.
Choose emotionally important experiences: graduation, wedding, funeral, first day at school, first sexual encounter, falling in love, losing someone. Begin visualizing, then support sensorially.

im_five_years_old_exercise.name: 'I'm Five Years Old' Exercise
im_five_years_old_exercise.goal: Access unconscious emotional storage from childhood to increase vulnerability, affectability, and depth of emotional availability.
im_five_years_old_exercise.how_to: State 'I'm five years old and I...' and stream consciousness from that age — places, people, experiences, feelings. Use first person present tense as much as possible to make it immediate. After exploring that age, advance year by year or jump to key ages. Do not censor or analyze what comes up.
im_five_years_old_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors; particularly those who have difficulty accessing vulnerability or childhood emotional states. Excellent for actors who feel disconnected from their own history.
im_five_years_old_exercise.why_it_works: Childhood experiences are stored in the unconscious with great emotional intensity. The exercise systematically unlocks these stored emotions, increasing the actor's vulnerability and range. Practice enriches dreams as a side effect.
im_five_years_old_exercise.additional_info: With practice, the content becomes deeper and reveals more vulnerability and emotional facets. Making it part of a daily workout builds a comfortable pathway to the unconscious.

sources.summary: Opens memory, frees forgotten emotions. Used for instrumental and craft purposes.
Example at age 5: living at an address, seeing Frankenstein, worrying. Then age 6: first grade, teacher's name, recess, toys.
Repeated practice: deeper content, more vulnerability, builds comfortable access to past. Also enriches dreams.

being_workout_exercise.name: Being Workout Exercise (Two-Person)
being_workout_exercise.goal: Eliminate self-consciousness and the obstacles to genuine relating by acknowledging them aloud to each other.
being_workout_exercise.how_to: Both actors sit or stand face to face. Begin speaking — honestly expressing what is happening in the moment. Name all obstacles: tension, self-consciousness, what you think of the other person, fears, attractions, repulsions, obligations. Respond to each other's statements authentically. Continue until both actors feel genuinely comfortable, related, and unselfconscious.
being_workout_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors; particularly essential before ensemble work and when self-consciousness is a major obstacle.
being_workout_exercise.why_it_works: Obstacles lose their power when named and expressed. The act of honest mutual disclosure reduces self-consciousness by making it the content of the relationship rather than a hidden obstacle.
being_workout_exercise.additional_info: May be followed by Dealing with the Demons for deeper clearing of specific blocks. Then move to selfless involvement exercises.

sources.summary: Face each other and communicate what's happening in the moment. Acknowledge all obstacles aloud. Continue until genuinely comfortable.
Sample dialogue shows how honest exchange of self-consciousness, curiosity, and attraction dissolves obstacles and creates genuine contact.

believability_exercise.name: Believability Preparation Exercise
believability_exercise.goal: Build an ensemble foundation by starting with real truths between the actors and gradually incorporating the fictional elements of the material.
believability_exercise.how_to: Identify what is truly real between the two actors that parallels the material (e.g., a real mutual attraction if the scene is a romantic encounter). Begin the improvisation starting from that true foundation. Express the real feelings honestly. Then add fictional elements from the scene or character one by one. Sprinkle the fiction into the truth rather than replacing it.
believability_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors doing two-person scene work; most powerful when there are genuine emotional parallels between the actors' real lives and the scene's content.
believability_exercise.why_it_works: Real feelings provide an authentic anchor; the vested interest created by genuine mutual engagement elevates moment-to-moment involvement and creates a natural ensemble dependency.
believability_exercise.additional_info: Most enjoyable of all ensemble preparations. Allows actors to express real feelings they've been reluctant to expose. Start with what is true — the fiction becomes believable when grounded in truth.

sources.summary: Start with what is true between the actors. Build outward to include untrue elements from the material.
Vested interest creates greater moment-to-moment involvement. Also allows expression of personal feelings previously unexpressed.

six_step_ensemble_preparation.name: Six-Step Ensemble Preparation Sequence
six_step_ensemble_preparation.goal: Systematically build from the actor's here-and-now reality to a full ensemble state ready to transition into the scene.
six_step_ensemble_preparation.how_to: 1. Personal Inventory — both actors simultaneously ask 'How do I feel?' 2. Relaxation exercises if tension exists as an obstacle. 3. Dealing with the Demons — articulate specific fears, obligations, competitive feelings. 4. Selfless Involvement — Being Workout, Wonder/Perceive/Observe, or Looking for Things Never Noticed. 5. Ensemble Preparation — one of: Intimate Sharing, Double Exposure, Wonder/Perceive/Observe, Telepathy, Blind Communication, Imaginary Monologues, Imaginary Dialogues, Believability, Ensemble Workout, or 'You Make Me Feel.' 6. Working for the Choice — overlap the ensemble preparation into the choice approach, then transition into the scene.
six_step_ensemble_preparation.who_should_do_it: All actors doing two-person scene work who want to achieve genuine ensemble. Most valuable for work that requires deep relating.
six_step_ensemble_preparation.why_it_works: Each step clears a different layer of obstacle, ensuring the actor is genuinely available at each level before moving to the next. The sequence builds momentum rather than requiring a cold start into deep work.
six_step_ensemble_preparation.additional_info: The steps can be abbreviated when the actors are experienced — they will feel when ensemble is reached. The key sign: no self-consciousness, moment-to-moment responsiveness, maximum unpredictability.

sources.summary: Six steps listed: Personal Inventory, relaxation, Dealing with Demons, selfless involvement, ensemble preparation, working for the choice.
Feel when ensemble is reached: no self-consciousness, comfortably relating, one impulse stimulated by another, maximum unpredictability. Then sneak into choices.

obligation_identification_exercise.name: Identifying the Obligations of Material
obligation_identification_exercise.goal: Map all the responsibilities of a piece of material before selecting choices and approaches.
obligation_identification_exercise.how_to: Read the material carefully and identify each of the seven obligation categories: time and place, relationship, character (with its four parts), emotional obligation, thematic, subtextual, and historic. For each obligation, note: what the playwright provides, what the character's perspective implies, and what the actor must create.
obligation_identification_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors approaching any scene or role. This is the fundamental craft starting point.
obligation_identification_exercise.why_it_works: Without clearly identifying what must be fulfilled, the actor cannot make intelligent choices. The obligations define the landscape; the choices and approaches are the tools used to navigate it.
obligation_identification_exercise.additional_info: The chart is built obligation by obligation. Time and place is often a good starting obligation. The psychological character element is often the most impelling and may be addressed first.

sources.summary: Three craft areas: obligations, choices, choice approaches. Identify obligations first.
Seven obligation types provide the complete framework for any piece of material.

character_element_exploration.name: Charting a Character in Four Areas
character_element_exploration.goal: Identify and analyze the character's physical, emotional, intellectual, and psychological elements, then map them to the actor's own similarities and differences.
character_element_exploration.how_to: Create a four-column chart: Physical, Emotional, Intellectual, Psychological. Fill in the character's description in each column from the text. Then note where you (the actor) are similar and where you are different. Start with similarities — use craft to bring those elements to the surface. For differences, select appropriate choices and approaches to bridge the gap.
character_element_exploration.who_should_do_it: All actors approaching any role. Especially important when character elements are far from the actor's natural range.
character_element_exploration.why_it_works: Starting from similarity is the most efficient path. The actor doesn't create from nothing but piques what is already within them. Differences are addressed through the craft system.
character_element_exploration.additional_info: One powerful choice (e.g., an affective memory) can sometimes fulfill multiple character element obligations simultaneously.

sources.summary: Four parts: physical, emotional, intellectual, psychological. Chart them all.
Begin with similarities, use craft to elevate them. Then address differences through specific choices and approaches.

sharing_monologue_exercise.name: Sharing Monologue Exercise
sharing_monologue_exercise.goal: Generate a specific emotional state before or during a scene by speaking (aloud or silently) about real personal experiences that parallel the scene's emotional obligations.
sharing_monologue_exercise.how_to: Identify the emotional obligation of the scene (e.g., depression, joy, grief, elation). Recall real experiences from your own life that contain or generated similar feelings. Speak about those experiences in detail, selectively emphasizing the elements that most powerfully generate the desired feeling. Can be done aloud before the scene or silently during it as an inner monologue.
sharing_monologue_exercise.who_should_do_it: All actors. Particularly accessible for beginners because it doesn't require complex sense memory — just honest sharing of real experience.
sharing_monologue_exercise.why_it_works: Real experiences carry real feelings. By selectively mining one's own history for parallel material, the actor creates genuine emotional life that directly addresses the scene's obligations.
sharing_monologue_exercise.additional_info: The selective emphasis is key — don't tell everything, choose the elements that most powerfully generate the desired state. Can transition seamlessly into an inner monologue during the scene itself.

sources.summary: Selectively emphasize the elements of real experience that parallel the scene's emotional obligation.
Example: actor becoming a father — used for elation. Example of depressive emphasis for 'night, Mother type material.

selective_enhancement_exercise.name: Selective Enhancement Exercise
selective_enhancement_exercise.goal: Amplify the emotional impact of real elements in the scene through sensory augmentation.
selective_enhancement_exercise.how_to: Focus on a specific real quality of the other actor (texture, scent, appearance, warmth, age). Begin responding to it as it actually is. Then, through sensory questions and responses, make it bigger, fuller, richer — add qualities not literally present but compatible with what is there. Continue to amplify until the element produces the desired emotional response.
selective_enhancement_exercise.who_should_do_it: Actors who respond well to sense memory work; actors working in scenes where the Available Stimulus is close to what is needed but not quite sufficient.
selective_enhancement_exercise.why_it_works: By amplifying real stimuli sensorially, the actor stays grounded in something genuine while enriching it beyond its literal properties — creating emotional responses more intense than the literal reality would produce.
selective_enhancement_exercise.additional_info: Works especially well in romantic scenes where genuine attraction exists but needs amplifying; also effective for creating feelings of grief, aging, or deep affection.

sources.summary: Example: enhance the texture of the other actor's lips (like velvet), their scent, the contrast of their features — making each element more vivid through sensory creation.

first_rehearsal_structure.name: First Rehearsal Structure
first_rehearsal_structure.goal: Establish a shared foundation of understanding, connection, and exploration for a new scene.
first_rehearsal_structure.how_to: 1. Read the play together or separately before meeting. 2. Optionally read the scene together — WITHOUT imposing any conceptual meaning. Read in stream-of-consciousness fashion; honor any emotional responses. 3. Discuss your respective interpretations to identify agreements and any differences. 4. Intimate Sharing — share real personal feelings to stimulate a deeper connection. 5. Set homework assignments. 6. Identify beginning obligations to explore in the next rehearsal.
first_rehearsal_structure.who_should_do_it: All actors beginning work on a new scene in a workshop or class setting.
first_rehearsal_structure.why_it_works: The first rehearsal is for open discovery, not delivery. By reading without concept, the material can carry the actors into responses. The Intimate Sharing establishes a genuine human connection before craft work begins.
first_rehearsal_structure.additional_info: Homework from the first rehearsal: one actor observes their own relationship for parallels to the scene; the other begins identifying specific obligations of the material.

sources.summary: Read in stream-of-consciousness fashion — no conceptual meaning imposed. Honor any emotional responses. Discuss interpretations afterward.
First rehearsal includes Intimate Sharing, selectively emphasizing personal relationship realities.

ten_point_film_preparation.name: Film Preparation Checklist
ten_point_film_preparation.goal: Ensure the actor arrives on a film set fully prepared for any scene they shoot.
ten_point_film_preparation.how_to: Before arriving on set: (1) Read the script; understand the character's arc. (2) Identify all obligations of your scene. (3) Create the time and place sensorially at home. (4) Work on character elements using appropriate choices and approaches. (5) Make decisions about which approaches to try. (6) Learn the lines thoroughly. On set: (7) Master the technical demands (camera frame, matching). (8) Create the place in the actual environment sensorially. (9) Work with the other actor if possible. (10) Understand the director's style and collaborate effectively.
ten_point_film_preparation.who_should_do_it: All actors working in film, especially those without formal rehearsal periods.
ten_point_film_preparation.why_it_works: Film provides no rehearsal safety net. The actor who arrives prepared with a craft can deliver genuine work under any technical constraints.
ten_point_film_preparation.additional_info: The actor who has done the home preparation can create the place on set much more quickly because most of the preparatory sensory work is already done.

sources.summary: Film requires the actor to come prepared having had only solitary involvement with the material.
Comprehensive list of requirements for film work: stay in touch with feelings at all times, use every second of rehearsal, prepare for the next day.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels disconnected or unaffectable — not moved by life, people, or scenes
recommendations.recommendation: Begin a daily consciousness-expansion practice: curiosity exercises, deep listening, personal-point-of-view work. Expand awareness of the world before trying to act in it.

sources.summary: Components of consciousness: curiosity, imagination, awareness, sensitivity, perception, interest, knowledge, affinity for life — all must be deliberately developed.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to access unconscious impulses in performance
recommendations.recommendation: First establish a solid being state, then work irreverently with choices. Expanded consciousness — through daily practice of its components — forms the bridge to unconscious impulses.

sources.summary: Three-stage chain: being → irreverence → ultimate consciousness. Cannot skip stages.
More conscious openness → greater access to unconscious impulses.

recommendations.condition: Actor has lost curiosity or feels bored by the world
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Curiosity Expansion Exercise: deliberately ask questions about everything, including things that seem uninteresting. Pursue deductive observation in public places.

sources.summary: Curiosity was suppressed by education and social pressure. It can be deliberately re-awakened by removing those barriers and questioning everything.

recommendations.condition: Actor struggles to listen to scene partners genuinely
recommendations.recommendation: Practice deep listening daily — dissect environmental sounds, listen for what people mean rather than what they say. Develop interest in everything people communicate verbally and vocally.

sources.summary: Listening reveals feeling, personality, and truth behind words. Train ears as instruments of deep reception.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to access unconscious material more consistently, not just by accident
recommendations.recommendation: Undertake the conditioning process: work daily with dream awareness practices, personal inventory, affective memory, and primal exercises. Consistency builds the conditioned conscious-unconscious channel.

sources.summary: The process of establishing the conscious-unconscious relationship is conditioned — daily work paves a road that can be traveled every time you act.
Cumulative process — each day's work adds layers. When the unconscious becomes consistent, the actor is multidimensional and inspired.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels their intuition is unreliable or nonexistent
recommendations.recommendation: Begin acknowledging and acting on intuitive feelings daily. Ask 'What do I feel about this person/situation?' and trust even fleeting responses. Track which intuitions prove accurate.

sources.summary: Intuition is conditioned knowledge, not mysterious gift. It is strengthened through use and acknowledgment.
Many intuitive impulses go unexpressed. Track and acknowledge them — strength comes from use.

recommendations.condition: Actor feels blocked by words — text feels intellectual rather than felt
recommendations.recommendation: Replace the written text with sounds or gibberish to find the emotional impulses, then use the Inner-Outer Monologue technique to reintegrate inner life with the written words.

sources.summary: Sounds and gibberish replace words, eliminating concepts and opening the actor to pure impulse. Inner-Outer Monologue reintegrates inner life with text.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs maximum emotional availability before a demanding scene with extreme emotional requirements
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Ultimate-Consciousness Trio: Primal Moan → Primitive Abandonment → Switch-Trick in Fear Areas, done consecutively without interruption. This is the most powerful single preparation sequence in Morris's system.

sources.summary: The Trio combines all three primal exercises. Done consecutively, the cumulative momentum produces amazing things from a very deep place.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to build a reliable library of choices that access the unconscious
recommendations.recommendation: Begin a Choice Hunt journal: catalogue all experiences, images, fears, and memories that produce strong emotional responses. Test each choice in acting work. Build this repertoire consistently — the larger and more tested it is, the more reliable unconscious access becomes.

sources.summary: Keep a journal listing all strong choices and underlining the ones that reliably access unconscious responses.

recommendations.condition: Actor needs to access extreme grief, terror, or helplessness for a role
recommendations.recommendation: Use Affective Memory, selecting the most powerful relevant past experience. Approach it through sensory re-creation of the surrounding context — do not attack the emotional moment directly. The Primal Moan can be done first to lower emotional defenses.

sources.summary: Re-create all surrounding context before approaching the emotional moment. All questions in the here and now.
Morris's own experience with Dark of the Moon: used affective memory of a death/terror experience — the fullest ultimate-consciousness experience he ever had.

recommendations.condition: Two actors need to achieve genuine ensemble before a scene and are starting from cold
recommendations.recommendation: Follow the six-step ensemble preparation sequence: Personal Inventory → Relaxation if needed → Dealing with the Demons → Selfless Involvement → Ensemble Preparation (choose the most appropriate exercise) → Working for the Choice. Don't rush — wait until both actors genuinely feel the ensemble state before transitioning to material.

sources.summary: The six steps provide a systematic pathway from wherever the actors are to a genuine ensemble state.

recommendations.condition: Actor keeps breaking ensemble when transitioning from preparation to the scene material
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Ensemble Workout's progressive structure (silence → sounds → gibberish → words) to make the transition gradual. Also, continue the ensemble preparation silently under the lines of the scene — use 'You Make Me Feel' and Working for a Choice simultaneously, without abruptly switching.

sources.summary: Continue preparation silently under lines to create a bridge between the exercise and the scene. The overlap prevents abrupt switching.
The Ensemble Workout's progressive structure avoids abrupt transitions — each part piggybacks the last.

recommendations.condition: Actor has difficulty maintaining moment-to-moment reality and keeps slipping into premeditated, performed responses
recommendations.recommendation: Return to the Being Workout — acknowledge what is actually happening in the moment, including the fact that you've lost contact. Use 'You Make Me Feel' to rebuild the chain of authentic reactions. Consider using the Believability exercise to re-anchor in real feelings.

sources.summary: When actors short-circuit moment-to-moment reality to service material cerebrally, they stop being real and must return to a genuine starting point.

recommendations.condition: Actor wants to build a comprehensive record of their process and choices
recommendations.recommendation: Start an Actor's Daily Journal: record everything of significance seen, felt, thought, and observed every day. Include the choices discovered, exercises done, observations about people, and catalogue of strong choices (especially those that access the unconscious). Carry it everywhere.

sources.summary: The journal chronicles stimuli, breaks them into choices, and serves as a blueprint of the daily work process.

recommendations.condition: Actor doesn't know what to do in a rehearsal
recommendations.recommendation: Use the Obligation-Choice-and-Choice-Approach Chart as the primary organizing framework. Start by identifying all the obligations of the material, then select choices and approaches for each. The chart provides a blueprint to follow from scene to scene.

sources.summary: The chart is a working document that maps obligations to choices and approaches — a clear blueprint for each rehearsal.

recommendations.condition: Actor struggles with a character very different from themselves
recommendations.recommendation: Start from the four-part character analysis. Find all points of similarity first — use craft to elevate those. Then address differences through the three most dimensional characterization approaches: Externals, Affective Memory, or Sub-Personalities.

sources.summary: Find similarities first. Use craft to bring them to the surface. Address differences through the appropriate choice approach.
The three most dimensional characterization approaches: Externals, Affective Memory, Sub-Personalities.

recommendations.condition: Actor is disconnected from their own childhood, making emotional access difficult
recommendations.recommendation: Use the 'I'm Five Years Old' journal — write the journal in first person at each age, beginning with the youngest accessible memory. This unlocks stored emotional experiences that increase vulnerability and availability.
